messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Online course: Species distribution and ecological niche modelling in R - September, 9th-13th
messages.subject_clean: Online course: Species distribution and ecological niche modelling in R - September, 9th-13th
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
registrations are now open for the upcoming edition of the online course "Species distribution and ecological niche modelling in R": [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/course45/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/course45/ )
 
Dates: September, 9th-13th
 
The course is aimed at students, researchers and practitioners at any career stage with an interest in building and applying species distribution and/or ecological niche models in a reproducible and automated way. Participants will build and validate models based on species occurrence data of their choice, and learn how to apply these models to a variety of purposes.
 
Previous basic experience with R is strongly desirable, though not strictly mandatory if you?re a fast learner. All R scripts will be provided and explained in detail.
 
For the full list of our courses and workshops, please visit: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/course45/ )
 
Best regards,
Carlo

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Only 5 Seats Left for Multivariate Data Analysis Course
messages.subject_clean: Only 5 Seats Left for Multivariate Data Analysis Course
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
Don't miss out on the opportunity to join our upcoming course on Multivariate Data Analysis with R and vegan! With only 5 seats remaining, secure your spot now for the session running from 27th to 31st May 2024.
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/vegan/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/vegan/ )
 
 
Course Overview:

In this comprehensive workshop, we'll delve into the practical applications of R and vegan for analyzing multivariate data sets. From ordination methods to restricted permutation tests, participants will gain valuable insights into statistical analysis techniques crucial for ecological research.
 
Target Audience and Assumed Background:

This course requires basic familiarity with RStudio and programming R code. Whether you're new to multivariate analysis or seeking to enhance your skills, this workshop offers a supportive learning environment.
 
Learning Outcomes:
By the end of the course, you will:

1. Gain a foundational understanding of multivariate analysis approaches.
2. Learn to select the most suitable method for your data set.
3. Master the use of restricted permutation tests for hypothesis testing.
4. Acquire proficiency in using R for multivariate data analysis.
 
Program:
Sessions will be held online, from 14:00 to 20:00 (Monday to Thursday) and 14:00 to 19:00 on Friday (Berlin time). Each session will blend lectures, practical exercises, and Q&A sessions to ensure an engaging learning experience.
 
 
For the full list of our courses and workshops, please visit: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/vegan/ )
 
 
Best regards,
Carlo
 
 
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

info at physalia-courses.org

mobile: +49 17645230846

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Calculating Bio climate variables (BIOVARS) in seasons
messages.subject_clean: Calculating Bio climate variables (BIOVARS) in seasons
messages.body_plain: I am running a MaxEnt model and we would like to see the seasonal pattern of the habitat suitability. Instead of doing the regular BIOVARS (R package) that calculate the annual bio-climate variables, we want to apply the similar concept and get the bio climate variable for just a season of 3-4 months. We are looking into BIO 6, BIO 13, and BIO 15 soecifically. However, the package biovars only take 12 layers (for the annual calculation). My understanding is that we can also interpret it for however long of period we would like (eg, 12 weeks and change the interpretation to 12 weeks of period instead of the annual definition in the example of R documentation). The only limitation is that the function needs to take 12 inputs. Is there a way to work around this to calculate the same variables but only for 3 or 4 months of period? All the temperature and precipitation variables are taken from remote sensing data that comes in different frequency (monthly & 8 days). Thank you !
messages.body_snippet: I am running a MaxEnt model and we would like to see the seasonal pattern of the habitat suitability. Instead of doing the regular BIOVARS (R package) that calculate the annual bio-climate variables,

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco]  Calculating Bio climate variables (BIOVARS) in seasons
messages.subject_clean: Calculating Bio climate variables (BIOVARS) in seasons
messages.body_plain:
Hello,

sure, the core idea behind of these bioclimatic variables is very simple:
bio6: apply(X = minimum.temperature.data.frame, MARGIN = 1, FUN = min)
bio13: apply(X = precipitation.data.frame, MARGIN = 1, FUN = max)
bio15: apply(X = precipitation.data.frame, MARGIN = 1, FUN = 
\(precipitations) return(sd(precipitations) / mean(precipitations)))
Of course, in the case of 8-days-resolution dataset, these functions 
calculate the minimum temperature of the 8-days periods, the 
precipitation of the wettest 8-days period, and the seasonality of the 
8-days periods. If you need months, then firstly you should aggregate 
your 8-days-resolution dataset to monthly resolution.
Also note that there are discrepancies between the definition/mane of 
bio6 ("minimum temperature of the coldest month", i.e., of the month 
that has the lowest monthly MEAN temperature) and the way it is usually 
calculated ("minimum of the monthly MINIMUM temperatures"). The two 
might differ.

This script might give you further help/ideas in this topic: 
https://github.com/bfakos/bioclimatic_variables/blob/main/calculate_BCVs.r

Hope this helps,
?kos
_____________
?kos Bede-Fazekas
Centre for Ecological Research, Hungary

2024. 05. 09. 7:22 keltez?ssel, Helen ?rta:
> I am running a MaxEnt model and we would like to see the seasonal pattern of the habitat suitability. Instead of doing the regular BIOVARS (R package) that calculate the annual bio-climate variables, we want to apply the similar concept and get the bio climate variable for just a season of 3-4 months. We are looking into BIO 6, BIO 13, and BIO 15 soecifically. However, the package biovars only take 12 layers (for the annual calculation). My understanding is that we can also interpret it for however long of period we would like (eg, 12 weeks and change the interpretation to 12 weeks of period instead of the annual definition in the example of R documentation). The only limitation is that the function needs to take 12 inputs. Is there a way to work around this to calculate the same variables but only for 3 or 4 months of period? All the temperature and precipitation variables are taken from remote sensing data that comes in different frequency (monthly & 8 days). Thank you !
> _______________________________________________
> R-sig-ecology mailing list
> R-sig-ecology at r-project.org
> https://stat.ethz.ch/mailman/listinfo/r-sig-ecology

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Hybrid course: Introduction to GAMM and GLMM with R - With GAM applications to spatial, and spatial-temporal data -
messages.subject_clean: Hybrid course: Introduction to GAMM and GLMM with R - With GAM applications to spatial, and spatial-temporal data -
messages.body_plain:
Hello,

We have a few remaining seats available on the following hybrid course 
(you can attended in person or simultaneously via Zoom):

Introduction to GAMM and GLMM with R - With GAM applications to spatial, 
and spatial-temporal data -.

When:? 24 - 28 June 2024
Where: University of Essex, Colchester, UK.
Course flyer: 
https://www.highstat.com/Courses/Flyers/Flyer2024_06_GLMMGAMM_Essex.pdf

Registration: www.highstat.com

*
*

*Outline:*

This course offers an introduction to generalised additive models 
(GAMs), linear mixed-effects models, generalised linear mixed-effects 
models (GLMMs), the combination of GAMs and GLMMs, and also includes 
examples of GAMMs applied to spatial and spatio-temporal data.


Kind regards,

Alain Zuur

www.highstat.com

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Extracting species loadings from gllvm::ordiplot()
messages.subject_clean: Extracting species loadings from gllvm::ordiplot()
messages.body_plain:
Dear list ? I am working with the gllvm package for the first time and trying to extract the loadings (coordinates) for both the latent variables and the species when biplot is set to TRUE in ordiplot(). I can get them for the latent variables with getLV.gllvm() but not the species. Is there any way to do this? Despite some searching I have not found a solution.

--

Jon Benstead
Professor
Department of Biological Sciences
University of Alabama, Box 870344
2099 Bevill Building, 201 7th Ave.
Tuscaloosa, Alabama 35487-0344

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Online course: Multivariate Data Analysis with R and vegan
messages.subject_clean: Online course: Multivariate Data Analysis with R and vegan
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,

there are the last seats remaining for our upcoming online course on Multivariate Data Analysis with R and vegan, taking place from May 27th to May 31st: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/vegan/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/vegan/ ) 

We'll delve into the powerful capabilities of R and the vegan package, focusing on ordination methods, diversity analysis, and ecological simulation. This course is designed to equip you with the essential tools to analyse complex data sets effectively.

Here's what you can expect to learn:

1. Gain a solid understanding of multivariate analysis approaches.
2. Learn to choose the right method for your data analysis needs.
3. Master the use of restricted permutation tests for analyzing predictor variables and experimental treatments.
4. Develop proficiency in utilizing R for multivariate data analysis.


Sessions will run from 2:00 PM to 8:00 PM (Monday to Thursday) and 2:00 PM to 7:00 PM on Friday, with dedicated Q&A and practical exercises each day.

Best regards,
Carlo

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Pairwise contrasts on univariate tests in mvabund::manyglm
messages.subject_clean: Pairwise contrasts on univariate tests in mvabund::manyglm
messages.body_plain:
Dear list:
I am using mvabund to analyze species counts from four sites sampled over three summers. Using pairwise.comp in anova.manyglm I can test for pairwise contrasts in the multivariate test. Is there any way to get the equivalent pairwise contrasts on all the univariate tests? Or would I have to do these separately?
Many thanks, Jon.

--

Jon Benstead
Professor
Department of Biological Sciences
University of Alabama, Box 870344
2099 Bevill Building, 201 7th Ave.
Tuscaloosa, Alabama 35487-0344

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] ONLINE COURSE – Adapting to the recent changes in R spatial packages (sf, terra, PROJ library) (PROJ05)
messages.subject_clean: ONLINE COURSE – Adapting to the recent changes in R spatial packages (sf, terra, PROJ library) (PROJ05)
messages.body_plain:
ONLINE COURSE ? Adapting to the recent changes in R spatial packages (sf,
terra, PROJ library) (PROJ05)

https://www.prstats.org/course/adapting-to-the-recent-changes-in-r-spatial-packages-sf-terra-proj-library-proj05/

We still have places on next week's course!

May 22nd-23rd 2024

Please feel free to share!

About this course:

R statistical software is becoming increasingly popular for spatial
analysis and mapping. This is partially due to a large number of R packages
devoted to applying various spatial methods. These packages, however, are
being revised, updated, or even superseded to allow for better performance,
simpler user interface, or expanded capabilities. Substantial recent
changes in R spatial packages include developing the ?sf? package as a
successor of ?sp?, creation of `terra` as a successor of `raster`, and
establishing the `stars` package. Additionally, all of these packages were
affected by the recent major updates of the PROJ library. In this course,
we will learn to use key packages for the analysis of spatial data, both
vector (?sf?) and raster (?terra?), and see how they differ from their
older counterparts, ?sp? and ?raster?. Another important aspect of the
course will be to understood spatial projections and coordinate systems,
how the recent PROJ changes affect R users, and how to adjust to them.

By the end of the course, participants should:

   - Understand the basic concepts behind spatial analysis ecosystem in R
   - Know how packages such as sp/rgeos/rgdal/raster differ from their
   successors sf/terra/star
   - Be able to switch from using packages such as sp/rgeos/rgdal/raster to
   sf/terra/stars
   - Understood the basic concepts behind spatial projections, and how
   PROJ.7 differs from PROJ4
   - Know how to deal with coordinate reference systems in R
   - Have the confidence to switch from PROJ4 to PROJ7 (i.e., for instance,
   adjusting old scripts based on PROJ4)?

Please email oliverhooker at prstatistics.com with any questions.

-- 

Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]
messages.body_snippet:

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] ONLINE COURSE – Multivariate Analysis Of Ecological Communities Using R With The VEGAN package (VGNR06) This course will be delivered live
messages.subject_clean: ONLINE COURSE – Multivariate Analysis Of Ecological Communities Using R With The VEGAN package (VGNR06) This course will be delivered live
messages.body_plain:
ONLINE COURSE ? Multivariate Analysis Of Ecological Communities Using R
With The VEGAN package (VGNR06) This course will be delivered live

https://www.prstats.org/course/multivariate-analysis-of-ecological-communities-using-r-with-the-vegan-package-vgnr06/
Please feel free to share!
18th - 22nd September

This 5-day course will cover R concepts, methods, and tools that can be
used to analyze community ecology data. The course will review data
processing techniques relevant to multivariate data sets. We will cover
diversity indices, distance measures and distance-based multivariate
methods, clustering, classification and ordination techniques using the R
package VEGAN. We will use real-world empirical data sets to motivate
analyses, such as describing patterns along gradients of environ-mental or
anthropogenic disturbances, quantifying the effects of continuous and
discrete predictors. We will emphasise visualisation and reproducible
workflows as well as good programming practices. The modules will consist
of introductory lectures, guided computer coding, and participant
exercises. The course is intended for intermediate users of R who are
interested in community ecology, particularly in the areas of terrestrial
and wetland ecology, microbial ecology, and natural resource management.
You are strongly encouraged to use your own data sets (they should be clean
and already structured, see the document: ?recommendation if you
participate with your data?.

Email oliverhooker at prstatistics.com with any questions

-- 

Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

With The VEGAN package (VGNR06) This course will be delivered live

https://www.prstats.org/course/multivariate-analysis-of-ecol

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Online Course: GLLVMs: Advanced Multivariate Analysis of Ecological Communities in R
messages.subject_clean: Online Course: GLLVMs: Advanced Multivariate Analysis of Ecological Communities in R
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 there are only 3 seats left for our upcoming course on "GLLVMs: Advanced Multivariate Analysis of Ecological Communities in R" scheduled for June 10-13.
 
This online course will explore Generalized Linear Latent Variable Models (GLLVMs) as a powerful framework for analyzing multivariate ecological data. The course will cover topics such as fitting GLLVMs with the gllvm R-package, formulating appropriate models, model checking and selection, and effective visualization and interpretation of results.
 
With only 3 seats remaining, this is your last chance to secure your spot! Whether you're a PhD candidate, postdoctoral researcher, or seasoned professional, this course offers invaluable insights and tools for advancing your research in ecological analysis.
 
Here are the key details:
Dates: June 10-13, 2024
Time: Classes from 2-8 PM Berlin time each day
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/gllvm/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/gllvm/ )
 
If you have any questions or need further assistance, feel free to reach out to us.
Best regards,
Carlo
 
 
--------------------

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Problem in Illustrating ZI GLMM
messages.subject_clean: Problem in Illustrating ZI GLMM
messages.body_plain:
Hello everyone,



I fitted a GLMM model on count data of the number of fruits produced by
a?a? palms in the Amazon using the glmmTMB package. The model is negative
binomial with zero inflation, a random structure that controls for repeated
measurements on the same palms in 2 consecutive years ("number" variable)
and a spatial structure of nested plots within transects within blocks. The
fixed part contains the year of measurement "ano_medida", the soil height
diameter of each plant das_mm and other variables linked to the hydrology
and forest structure of each plot. The variables in the fixed part were
z-standardized to stabilize the model and deal with overdispersion. The
model was able to deal with an overdispersion problem and a high proportion
of structural zeros. The selection was made first for the zero-inflation
part and then for the conditional part.



I would like to graphically illustrate the relationship of the non zero
inflated part between n_fruits and das_mm conditional on the results found
in the model. I tried to do this using the visreg function but I get a
repeated error:



> visreg(modelo16_REML, "das_mm", type = "contrast", data =
dados_transformados)

Error in eval(predvars, data, env) :

  objeto 'dif_alt_dossel_m' n?o encontrado



I tried to apply a solution I found in the visreg FAQ, but it didn't work:



fit$gam does not include the call (i.e., fit$gam$call is NULL), which means
visreg won?t be able to find the data:

visreg <https://pbreheny.github.io/visreg/reference/visreg.html>(fit$gam,
'x')

# Error in FUN(X[[i]], ...): object 'y' not found

So you have to include it manually:

fit$gam$data <- Data

visreg <https://pbreheny.github.io/visreg/reference/visreg.html>(fit$gam,
'x')



modelo16_REML$data = dados_transformados



However visreg did not work in this way also.



Can anyone tell me how to illustrate this relationship? I'm only referring
to the conditional part of the model, because I'm not going to illustrate
the zeros inflation part, it was only used to control the zeros inflation
part and it was illustrated separately through a logistic model, for which
visreg worked well.



Thanks in advance for any ideas,



Alexandre



modelo16_REML <-

  glmmTMB(n_frutos ~

            ano_medida + das_mm +

            (1|numero) + (1|bloco/transecto/parcela),

          ziformula = ~ ano_medida +

            das_mm + dif_alt_dossel_m + elevation_m + hand_m +
basal_area_m2 + tree_density +

            das_mm:dif_alt_dossel_m + elevation_m:hand_m +

            (1|numero) + (1|bloco/transecto/parcela),

          family = nbinom1,

          data = dados_transformados, REML=TRUE)



> summary(modelo16_REML)

 Family: nbinom1  ( log )

Formula:

n_frutos ~ ano_medida + das_mm + (1 | numero) + (1 |
bloco/transecto/parcela)

Zero inflation:

~ano_medida + das_mm + dif_alt_dossel_m + elevation_m + hand_m +

    basal_area_m2 + tree_density + das_mm:dif_alt_dossel_m +

    elevation_m:hand_m + (1 | numero) + (1 | bloco/transecto/parcela)

Data: dados_transformados



     AIC      BIC   logLik deviance df.resid

 22121.3  22255.7 -11036.7  22073.3     1975



Random effects:



Conditional model:

 Groups                  Name        Variance Std.Dev.

 numero                  (Intercept) 0.019859 0.14092

 parcela:transecto:bloco (Intercept) 0.006741 0.08211

 transecto:bloco         (Intercept) 0.015453 0.12431

 bloco                   (Intercept) 0.020497 0.14317

Number of obs: 1995, groups:

numero, 761; parcela:transecto:bloco, 146; transecto:bloco, 39; bloco, 10



Zero-inflation model:

 Groups                  Name        Variance  Std.Dev.

 numero                  (Intercept) 1.323e+00 1.1500780

 parcela:transecto:bloco (Intercept) 4.588e-01 0.6773243

 transecto:bloco         (Intercept) 9.890e-07 0.0009945

 bloco                   (Intercept) 1.970e-01 0.4438035

Number of obs: 1995, groups:

numero, 761; parcela:transecto:bloco, 146; transecto:bloco, 39; bloco, 10



Dispersion parameter for nbinom1 family ():  598



Conditional model:

               Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)

(Intercept)     8.61419    0.05588  154.16  < 2e-16 ***

ano_medida2017 -0.32062    0.02686  -11.94  < 2e-16 ***

ano_medida2018 -0.47854    0.02501  -19.13  < 2e-16 ***

das_mm          0.08344    0.01869    4.46 8.04e-06 ***

---

Signif. codes:  0 ?***? 0.001 ?**? 0.01 ?*? 0.05 ?.? 0.1 ? ? 1



Zero-inflation model:

                        Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)

(Intercept)             -0.97345    0.22911  -4.249 2.15e-05 ***

ano_medida2017           1.82993    0.18150  10.082  < 2e-16 ***

ano_medida2018          -0.01319    0.17647  -0.075   0.9404

das_mm                  -1.24866    0.13190  -9.467  < 2e-16 ***

-- 
Dr. Alexandre F. Souza
Professor Associado

Universidade Federal do Rio Grande do Norte

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] TIME SENSITIVE | Online Course: Intro to R and Data Manipulation & Analysis
messages.subject_clean: TIME SENSITIVE | Online Course: Intro to R and Data Manipulation & Analysis
messages.body_plain:
Dear All,

Ready to take your data analysis skills to the next level? Whether you're a
student, researcher, or professional, our Beginner's R Workshop is made for
you! Join us online on May 18th and 19th (11 AM IST to 1 PM IST).

Here's what you can expect from our workshop:

*Fundamentals of R Programming:* Learn the basics of R programming language
from scratch.

*Efficient Data Manipulation:* Discover techniques to manipulate data
efficiently and effectively.

*Expert Guidance:* Our experienced instructors will provide step-by-step
guidance to help you build confidence and proficiency in using R for your
projects.

*Online Session Fees:*

?  Professionals: INR 1500

?  Researchers: INR 1300

?  Students: INR 1000

*Register now* for the online session using the following Google Form link:
https://forms.gle/1QQryTZw371S1xjW8


Reserve your spot today and unlock the full potential of R! Got questions
or need assistance? Feel free to reach out to us at akshatakarnik at gmail.com,
and we'll be happy to help.

Don't miss out on this opportunity to embark on your data analysis journey.
Sign up now and secure your spot!



Best regards,
Akshata Karnik

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Pairwise contrasts on univariate tests in mvabund::manyglm
messages.subject_clean: Pairwise contrasts on univariate tests in mvabund::manyglm
messages.body_plain:
Hi Jon,
I suggest you look In David Warton?s new book, Eco-Stats: Data Analysis in Ecology (2023).  He is one of the coauthors of ?mvabund.?  Part III covers Multivariate Abundances, starting with Chapter 14.  Exercise 14.1 is an example examining whether there are difference in the invertebrate communities in a revegetation study.  Section 14.2 applies GEE (General Estimating Equations) for the multivariate invertebrate community data using the mvabund library (Code Box 14.1).  In section 14.4, they do post-hoc univariate testing of the invertebrate community data, with adjusting P-values for multiple testing to control family-wise Type I error.  So, perhaps this approach also will work for your data, also.

Good luck!

Best Regards,

Carl von Ende
cvonende at niu.edu<mailto:cvonende at niu.edu>

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Question on R GIS packages to extract point associations
messages.subject_clean: Question on R GIS packages to extract point associations
messages.body_plain:
Hi all,

Eons ago Hawthorne Beyer had Hawths Tools that used R to provide some 
really useful things like split shape files such as the IUCN or Mammals 
of the World into species specific files.? This has long since been 
defunct >15 years.? I do not recall but I think there was also an 
association module that was able to extract polygon overlay features 
related to the point data.

I can accomplish what I am interested in using ESRI ArcGIS or ArcPro but 
it takes a lot of time to set this up. Then run this one species at a 
time using Xtools.

What I need to do is extract species associations (point data) from 
multiple layers of shape file polygons that are varied ecological 
attributes.
This extracts all of the attributes of the overlays for each point layer.
Examples are elevation, vegetation types, ecosystem etc. then write the 
table of all associations by species.
Often I need to include proximity to water bodies like rivers, lakes and 
lagoons.

Are/Is there an R package or packages that can do this w/o loading the 
GIS and using the point data and ecological attribute polygons using R?

I have hundreds of species I need to do this with at varied geographic 
levels, both country level and landscape level like Mesoamerica, 
Caribbean etc.

Thanks to any R GIS gurus other there for a reply.

Bruce Miller

Eons ago Hawthorne Beyer had Hawths Tools that used R to provide some

of the World into species specific files.? This

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Question on R GIS packages to extract point associations
messages.subject_clean: Question on R GIS packages to extract point associations
messages.body_plain:
Hi Bruce,

If I understand your needs correctly, I think you can get it done with the
extract() function from package {terra} by looping over all your species
and all attributes per species.

I once extracted distribution range metrics for ~2k mammal species from the
IUCN data using extract() from the **{raster}** package, which took about
3-4 days IIRC, but {terra} functions are generally faster than their
{raster} ancestors (and your code may well be more efficient than mine!).

Hope this helps,
Roi

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Question on R GIS packages to extract point associations
messages.subject_clean: Question on R GIS packages to extract point associations
messages.body_plain:
Yes, you can attribute information from polygon (or raster) data into point datasets.
And, yes, you can script this to run for multiple species at once, once you get it set up.

You probably should start with the `sf` package (https://r-spatial.github.io/sf/) and
look at `st_intersects`. There's even an example of finding points that fall within polygons
in the help section for that call (https://r-spatial.github.io/sf/reference/geos_binary_pred.html).
Also see `st_join`.

If you want to find proximity to a feature such as water, then you probably want to
generate a distance-to-water raster and intersect your points with the raster. Again
you can do that all in R, but there's a little more prep involved.

I remember Hawth's Tools well!  Thanks for the trip down memory lane.

Cheers,
Tim

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Course: Generalised Linear Mixed Models in R
messages.subject_clean: Course: Generalised Linear Mixed Models in R
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
registrations are open for the 3rd edition of the Physalia course on "Generalised Linear Mixed Models in R".
 
Dates: online, October 21st-24th
 
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/glmms-in-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/glmms-in-r/ )
 
This course is aimed at graduate students and researchers who have experience with generalized linear regression models in R and want to extend their knowledge by learning how to add random effects, correlation structures, and variance models to these models. The basics of LM, GLM, and ANOVA are reviewed at the beginning of the course.
 
After attending this course, you will be able to:
 
1. Deepen your understanding of fundamental regression concepts, including centering, scaling, interactions, contrasts, and ANOVA. 2. Understand the components of the GLMM framework (choice of distributions, random effects, variance structures, correlation structures) 3.  Choose the appropriate model structure in an applied analysis of experimental or observational data (focusing on the R packages lme4 and glmmTMB)4. Interpret the fitted models and calculate the expected effect of predictors on the response. 5. Know how to visualize fitted GLMMs (R package effects) and to check the assumptions of the model (R package DHARMa)
 
 
For the full list of our courses and workshops, please visit: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/glmms-in-r/ )
 
Best regards,
Carlo
 
 
 
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

info at physalia-courses.org

mobile: +49 17645230846

Dear all,
We have one last seat available for our upcoming online course, "Ecological Time Series Analysis and Forecasting in R," taking place next week - 27-31 May.
Course website:[ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/time-series-in-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/time-series-in-r/ )
This course offers a unique opportunity to delve into advanced time series analysis tailored for ecological data, using powerful Bayesian modelling tools like Stan, {mvgam}, and {brms}. Enhance your skills in wrangling, visualizing, and analyzing ecological time series to gain valuable insights and produce accurate forecasts.
Don't miss out on this chance to join a global community of researchers and elevate your expertise. Register now to secure the final spot!

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] ONLINE COURSE – Time Series Analysis and Forecasting using R and Rstudio (TSAF01)
messages.subject_clean: ONLINE COURSE – Time Series Analysis and Forecasting using R and Rstudio (TSAF01)
messages.body_plain:
ONLINE COURSE ? Time Series Analysis and Forecasting using R and Rstudio
(TSAF01)

https://www.prstats.org/course/time-series-analysis-and-forecasting-using-r-and-rstudio-tsaf01/

Instructor - Dr. Rafael De Andrade Moral

17th - 26th October

Please feel free to share!

In this six-day course (Approx. 35 hours), we provide a comprehensive
practical and theoretical introduction to time series analysis and
forecasting methods using R. Forecasting tools are useful in many areas,
such as finance, meteorology, ecology, public policy, and health. We start
by introducing the concepts of time series and stationarity, which will
help us when studying ARIMA-type models. We will also cover autocorrelation
functions and series decomposition methods. Then, we will introduce
benchmark forecasting methods, namely the na?ve (or random walk) method,
mean, drift, and seasonal na?ve methods. After that, we will present
different exponential smoothing methods (simple, Holt?s linear method, and
Holt-Winters seasonal method). We will then cover autoregressive integrated
moving-average (or ARIMA) models, with and without seasonality. We will
also cover Generalized Additive Models (GAMs) and how they can be used to
incorporate seasonality effects in the analysis of time series data.
Finally, we will cover Bayesian implementations of time series models and
introduce extended models, such as ARCH, GARCH and stochastic volatility
models, as well as Brownian motion and Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes.

Please email oliverhooker at prstatistics.com with any questions.
--

-- 

Oliver Hooker PhD.
PR stats

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]
messages.body_snippet:
ONLINE COURSE ? Time Series Analysis and Forecasting using R and Rstudio
(TSAF01)

https://www.prstats.org/course/time-series-analysis-and-forecasting-using-r-and-rstudio-tsaf01/

Instructor - Dr. Raf

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Join Us Online for  the course "Interactive Maps with R" – June 17-18
messages.subject_clean: Join Us Online for  the course "Interactive Maps with R" – June 17-18
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
We are excited to announce our upcoming course, "Interactive Maps with R," taking place online on June 17-18.
This course will teach you how to create and customize interactive maps using R, with practical hands-on exercises and theoretical insights.
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/interactive-maps-with-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/interactive-maps-with-r/ )
 
The course is entirely based on free and open-source R software, with packages like ?leaflet? and ?mapview?. Classes will include some theoretical lectures, but mostly practical hands-on exercises. Participants will be given annotated R scripts to create different types of interactive maps with provided data. They will then create their own interactive maps using their newly acquired data and skills.
 
 
Best regards,
Carlo
 
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

info at physalia-courses.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-eco] Correct use of blocks in mvabund::anova.manyglm
messages.subject_clean: Correct use of blocks in mvabund::anova.manyglm
messages.body_plain:
Dear list: I am analyzing community data in which we have 6 samples per date across 6 dates per year at 4 sites over 3 years. Is it appropriate to permute across all rows in such an analysis? Or should I be restricting the permutation in some way (i.e. block by date)? I realize that this might be my call, but I was hoping to get some advice, philosophical or otherwise. I am new to mvabund and have done some research but there are still few examples out there. Yours, Jon.

--

Jon Benstead
Professor
Department of Biological Sciences
University of Alabama, Box 870344
2099 Bevill Building, 201 7th Ave.
Tuscaloosa, Alabama 35487-0344
